speaker: interviewer
text: Today is March 18, 1997. This is an interview with Rose Patoka in Baltimore, Maryland, United States of America. The interviewer will be Marcie Goodman. And this is being done in English. Today is march 18, 1997. This is an interview with Mrs. Rose Patoka in Baltimore, Maryland, United States of America. The interviewer is Marcie Goodman. And this will be conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewee
text: Good afternoon.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you please tell me your first and last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My first name is Rose, and my last name Patoka.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you spell Patoka for me, please?

speaker: interviewee
text: P-A-T-O-K-A.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your name at birth, your maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prager. P-R-A-G-E-R, Prager.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do you have a Hebrew name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shoshanah, Rose Shoshanah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your date of birth?

speaker: interviewee
text: October 24, 1925.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old are you today?

speaker: interviewee
text: 71, I think. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you born?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was born in Holoby.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a little town near Kowel.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you say near Kowel being the largest city to--

speaker: interviewee
text: The largest city, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --to-- are you saying Holovy?

speaker: interviewee
text: Holoby.

speaker: interviewer
text: Holoby.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was your father's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My father's name was Israel. Yehuda.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he born in the same small-- in Holoby.

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He was born in [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: Near Holoby?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's not far. Yeah, near. Yeah, Holoby.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. What did your father do for a living?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, before he got married or after?

speaker: interviewer
text: After he was married.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he went to villages and bought products like chickens, eggs, and brought it to the big city. Farm products, he brought them to the big city.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And how about your mother? What was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Bunia.

speaker: interviewer
text: And her maiden name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her maiden name was Leikach, L-E-I-K-A-C-H.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was she born in Poland also?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. She was born in Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know what town?

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have brothers and sisters?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had, now I don't have it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. How many children were there in your family?

speaker: interviewee
text: Seven. I had one sister and five brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you tell me, starting with the oldest to the youngest, their names?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mhm. OK, the oldest was my sister. Her name was Chaya. The younger ones were brothers-- Moshe, Avrum, Nachman, Lev, and Shalom.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where were you in the line of age?

speaker: interviewee
text: Between-- between Nachman and Lev.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And where did you go to school?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went to school in Holoby.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type of school? Was it public?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a public school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So does that mean they were Jewish and non-Jewish children in the school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And was it elementary school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Like elementary school, high school, all that together.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far was the school from your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, within walking distance. I walked to school.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was the school day like? Was it a long day at school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We started at like 8 o'clock, 9 o'clock. We finished at 3:00.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have lunch at school? Or did you come home for lunch?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, sometimes we had lunch at school. Sometimes we brought lunch, and we ate our own lunch.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you have a Jewish education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish education I had from a private teacher.

speaker: interviewer
text: That came to your home?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] It was like a Yiddish school. A private school.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you went after the other school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your brothers and sisters all go to that same school? The Yiddish school?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not all. No. No, my brothers and sisters-- they were boys.

speaker: interviewer
text: Other than your one sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: My sister, I don't remember where she went. The boys went to cheder, to Jewish school, and the synagogue. They were boys. It was different.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, was your sister married when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. None of the children were married yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you describe for me what Holoby was like? Was it a large town?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a small town near Holoby-- near Kowel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were many of the families, or most of the families, in the town Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lot of the Jewish people, but not most of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have a synagogue in the town?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. We had a synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it the only synagogue?

speaker: interviewee
text: The only one. It was an old synagogue, a beautiful synagogue from the old times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember who the rabbi might have been?

speaker: interviewee
text: The rabbi was from Poland. I forgot his name. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: I forgot his name. He was from Poland. And what else was in the town? Was there a mikvah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, there was a mikvah. Near the shul was a mikvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were a lot of the businesses owned by the Jewish families, would you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mostly yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So would the Jewish people have lived in the same part of the town? Did they live separately?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, the same part of the town.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was your family religious?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you say yes, does that mean that they were Orthodox or Hasidic?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, my father was Hasidic really. He belonged to the rabbi in-- the rabbi in Stolin. Stolin. He was a Stolin Hasid. He used to go to the rabbi in Stolin.

speaker: interviewer
text: How far would that have been from you, do you think?

speaker: interviewee
text: I come here maybe like from here to Chicago or something. I don't know exactly. But that's--

speaker: interviewer
text: He never took you with him?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. [LAUGHS] I was a girl on top of it. You know he didn't take me.

speaker: interviewer
text: So would your father have gone to shul every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did he daven every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: He daven every day.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, did you go to synagogue on Shabbos for services? Or the girls really didn't do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: On holidays maybe. Rosh Hashanah, Yom Kippur. Because we were girls. Girls didn't come.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother wear a sheitel?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Can you describe what your own house was like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My house?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, what was it like?

speaker: interviewee
text: My house was good. [LAUGHS] Because I was young then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember how many rooms were in the house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rooms?

speaker: interviewer
text: Or was it--

speaker: interviewee
text: Wait a minute. One, two, three. Like four rooms and a kitchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have to share a bedroom with--

speaker: interviewee
text: With my sister, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what the address was of your house.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was number one [NON-ENGLISH]. That means [NON-ENGLISH] street. You know, [NON-ENGLISH] was a Polish fighter. Do you know about that or not?

speaker: interviewer
text: No, I don't.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's why the street was named after him. He was like a Polish-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] --like a general or something in the old times.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was your street.

speaker: interviewee
text: That was my street. [NON-ENGLISH], yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did you have a yard that you played in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we had a little garden and a little small yard to play there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was it like for you being the youngest girl with all those brothers?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was all right. Because in our family, the oldest one was a girl. And I was a girl. The rest were brothers.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help your mother a lot with the housework and the cooking?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not too much. Because my older sister did that.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. Now, did you have any relatives that lived in Holoby? Did you have grandparents living when you grew up?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had one grandmother. That's all I knew, a grandmother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who's mother was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not a grandfather.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your mother's or your father?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was my mother's mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her name was Hanna. The last time was Leikach.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did she also live in Holoby?

speaker: interviewee
text: She lived in Holoby.

speaker: interviewer
text: But not with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: We lived on one side of the railroad track. She lived on the other side of the railroad track.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you see much of your grandmother?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I saw her.

speaker: interviewer
text: And were there other relatives like aunts and uncles?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. I had a lot of uncles and aunts. They all were named Leikach. Not on my father's side. I didn't no nobody. But on my mother's side, Leikach.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did they mostly live in Holoby?

speaker: interviewee
text: They mostly lived in Holoby. Whoever's left living now is living here in Baltimore.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And were there any relatives that you had that lived outside of Poland? Did you any relatives that lived in other countries?

speaker: interviewee
text: I had United States relatives like an uncle-- an uncle, aunt that lived in the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what language would you have spoken at home?

speaker: interviewee
text: At home? Well, at home, we spoke Yiddish. But I knew Polish, Ukrainian, German, a little bit of Hebrew, not that much. And that's it. From every language, a little bit-- like Spanish, Italian, this, that, a little bit of everything.

speaker: interviewer
text: Please tell me about your childhood. What type of child were you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, we were poor like the devil. We had nothing. But it was a pretty good childhood. I went to school, played at lunch with friends. That's all I can say. Beyond, not too much.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you do you remember the names of some of your friends from childhood?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some of them. One was Sonia next door. She was a beautiful girl. The Germans killed her. And a lot of them that went to school with me. I don't remember names now.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were any of the children you were friendly with not Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. There were some. Some of them were not Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would those non-Jewish friends have come to your house?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sometimes. I had one Polish girl. She was a friend of me. Her father was something in the-- what do you call the royal family?

speaker: interviewer
text: I forgot what it's called in English. Like from a royal family. How do you call in English? Well, from the royal family anyway. He was like an administrator or something. She used to talk me into becoming not Jewish.

speaker: interviewee
text: [LAUGHS] She said, I got blonde hair. My hair was lighter than now.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were blonde when you were--

speaker: interviewee
text: I was blonde, dark blonde. And she said, I talk so good Polish. She talked me into stop being Jewish, become Polish. I never listened to her. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Was there anything particular that you liked to do as a child? Did you have any special hobby or interest?

speaker: interviewee
text: I wanted to be a teacher. But I never pursued it.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Please tell me what Jewish holidays were like. You had a large family. You said that you had a lot of aunts and uncles. So for instance, on Jewish holidays, would you have been with a lot of the family, like at Passover or Hanukkah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, well, Passover, Hanukkah, everybody has in his house because there wasn't enough food to feed the other families. But it was pretty good like Purim. We used to go get dressed up for Purim.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then did you go to the synagogue? Is that where you would go?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we went to synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they have like a Purim celebration there?

speaker: interviewee
text: A Purim celebration. Well, and Yom Kippur, Rosh Hashanah, all them holidays. See, the parents and the children went to school. And they had kind of in the middle. We read books. We thought of different things. But the parents, their whole life, all of the parents, the whole life was the synagogue. And everything, all the celebrations and everything was centered around the synagogue.

speaker: interviewer
text: When your family made Shabbos, for instance, did your mother make a special Shabbos dinner?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. She made a [NON-ENGLISH] and a [NON-ENGLISH]. And Sabbath was the main celebration.

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't necessarily go to synagogue for Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Your father and the boys went.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was a special time in the house for you, Shabbos?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, yeah. We didn't go to school. We played Shabbos. [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family put up a sukkah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you help decorate it? Would you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. I remember the roof was made out of sunflower. Not the seeds, the sticks, the branches--

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: --on the sunflower things. We decorated the sukkah. We ate in the sukkah. It was kind of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Would all the Jewish families have had a sukkah?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: That would have been a typical thing?

speaker: interviewee
text: So that time, there wasn't non-religious. Everybody was religious. The whole life was centered around religion.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And what would you say the relationship was like as you were growing up between the Jews and the non-Jews in Holoby?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Holoby? Very few that were good with Jews. The rest of them were antisemites. They were hatin Jews.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you ever feel that you were the target of antisemitic remarks? I mean, did you experience antisemitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, not really. Not me. Not really, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Did your family ever that you were aware of?

speaker: interviewee
text: My family? [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] No. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Really not.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. Now, the part of Poland that you lived in was occupied by the Russians in 1939?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1939, the Russians came there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did living under that occupation affect life? Did your life change when they came in in '39?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came in, they were picking on the Jews that were richer. They weren't picking on us because we weren't rich. We were poor anyway. And so it didn't affect us, not really when the Russians came.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they deport or arrest Jews that you knew of? Did the Russians do that? Did they arrest anyone because they were Jewish?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was-- was-- what was the effect of the Russian occupation on Jewish life? Were you still able to go to the synagogue and celebrate holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, we could celebrate the holidays. But-- --it was forbidden. The trade was forbidden. They couldn't do so much with business. Because they were against all that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Could you still go for your Hebrew education?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, you could. You still could.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall, for instance, any of your brothers being bar mitzvahed? Do you have any memory of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, at that time, they didn't make no big bar mitzvahs like they do here. They went in the shul. They had a bottle of whiskey and a few cakes. And that was the-- mostly, those cakes-- how do you call them? They sell them here. [NON-ENGLISH]. That's all. They had a bottle of whiskey and some of them [NON-ENGLISH]. And that's it. That was bar mitzvah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your mother bake those?

speaker: interviewee
text: Some, yeah, most of it my mother did.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. And when did you first learn about Hitler's rise to power?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, right away, we read in the papers in 1933.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you recall being aware, for instance, of Kristallnacht, what had happened in Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: We didn't exactly know that. We found that out later.

speaker: interviewer
text: When the Russians came, did Jewish refugees come from other parts of Poland to where you lived?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had they been--

speaker: interviewee
text: Some people came from the Germans. They ran away from the Germans and from deep Poland.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what did they tell you? Do you remember hearing stories from them?

speaker: interviewee
text: They told us a lot that the Germans were taking advantage of them. They had to run away. They didn't talk too much, just whatever you hear, the little bit.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did your family talk about what was happening in Germany to the Jews? Do you remember them discussing? Were they afraid of the Germans coming to Poland?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not really. Not really because nobody knew for sure what happened.

speaker: interviewer
text: So from '39 to '41, that was when you lived under the Russian occupation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: At that time, were any of your-- you said your sister was not married. Were any of your brothers married then?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your--

speaker: interviewee
text: They worked. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did the Germans actually come to Holoby?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember around when? Was it spring?

speaker: interviewee
text: In June. In June.

speaker: interviewer
text: In June. And do you recall seeing them for the first time?

speaker: interviewee
text: The Germans? No, I never saw them.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never saw them?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, I never saw the Germans.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened when they came to you?

speaker: interviewee
text: When they came, see, I ran away two days before they came in our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: You ran away?

speaker: interviewee
text: I ran away to Russia two days before they got into our town.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did that come about that you left for Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because all the youth was leaving, the young people. And everybody was afraid. Maybe it was something in school, maybe this, maybe that. Everybody ran away, the young ones.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you say young, what age group are you talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: --like from 10 to 20. So did you talk about that with your family that you were going to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. We didn't talk about it. We just picked ourselves up and run.

speaker: interviewer
text: And who did you go with?

speaker: interviewee
text: I went with my older brother Nachman and my younger brother Lev.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you mean that the three of you just decided we're going to run away?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're going to Russia, yeah, with all the rest of them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, were both of your parents living at the time?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. Just one, the father lived.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what had happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died when the Soviets were there in 1940.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was she killed?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, no. She died. She was sick.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So the three of you never talked to your father or the others.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: You just decided to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you make any--

speaker: interviewee
text: See, my brother Avrum was already in the Russian army. They took him because his year, he was born in 1919. And his year, they took. They mobilized. That's why he was in the Russian army.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was he in touch with you by sending letters?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you knew where he was in Russia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Later, he was in jail. But at the beginning, he was in the army. And my oldest brother was some big shot on the railroad. He ran away separate. And then me and my brother older than me and the one younger than me, we ran away together.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then who was left home? It was your sister.

speaker: interviewee
text: My father, my sister, my youngest brother.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so it was your father. And you said Chaya and Shalom?

speaker: interviewee
text: Chaya and Shalom and my father. They were killed with the Nazis.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So when you left, did you take anything with you? Were you able to?

speaker: interviewee
text: What I took with me was my dress and my shoes. That's all.

speaker: interviewer
text: I mean, what you had on was all that--

speaker: interviewee
text: What I had on. That's all. What I had on.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you get to Russia? How did you get from Holoby to--

speaker: interviewee
text: We went-- first, we went on horse and wagon, a little walking. That's how all the people went, the refugees.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you said it was all the young people, was it a big group? I mean, how many people would you say it was that left?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was a lot of people together, even Ukrainian people. But then the Ukrainians run back because they were afraid of the Germans. They run back, the Ukrainians. And the Jews went all over everywhere.

speaker: interviewer
text: Would you say it was a large group of young Jewish people?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe 100 to 150 maybe. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you recall that day when you left, what you were thinking? Did you think you'd be coming back in a short while?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were thinking-- at that time, we were thinking they're going to run out the Germans real fast. So we're going to run back home. But it was just a dream. We just thought that.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it sad for you leaving? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: Sure, it was sad. I left my father and my sister and my little brother. The older brothers, I knew they were OK. But my father, my sister, and my little brother, I was scared of what happened to them. I remember my father was standing in the middle of the city. And somebody threw a harmonica on his hand. He was holding it and crying. He was crying and holding that harmonica. I remember that.

speaker: interviewer
text: The day that you were leaving?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that when you last saw him, standing there?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's when I last saw him. That's when I last saw him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he knew that you were leaving with your two brothers at that point.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, sure. Because he didn't know if there was any other way to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he did not want to leave?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. He didn't want to leave. So my sister and my little brother. They were left.

speaker: interviewer
text: How old was the youngest at the time? How old was your brother, the little one?

speaker: interviewee
text: The youngest one? He was about maybe nine or 10, something like that.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how how long of a trip was it 'til you got to-- was it days until you got to Russia? How long did it take?

speaker: interviewee
text: Maybe-- maybe a week or two. I'm not sure. We went fast to Kiev. Wait, then Solina. Solina, that's on the border of Russia and Poland. They took away my brother to army, Nachman.

speaker: interviewer
text: When you got to the border?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because how old was he then?

speaker: interviewee
text: He was almost 20 then. I was-- he was about three or four years older than me. He was maybe 20 or something.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when you got to the border, the Russians--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: --said you're needed for the army?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know if they took him or he went himself maybe. Because that time, it was-- my head was-- I don't know how you call it.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then-- who were you with then? Your brother Lev?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with my brother Lev.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you two go?

speaker: interviewee
text: We went farther, wherever it took us, wherever. We got on a train there. And wherever the train went, that's where we went.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: At first, we went Kiev. Kid From Kiev, we went on a boat on the Dnieper. There's a river, Dnieper, between Kiev and Dnepropetrovsk. We got on that boat. And from that boat, we went on a train again. We went to--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Rostov Oblast. That's on the Don. I don't know if you know it or not. On the Don, we went to Taganrog. We been in a [NON-ENGLISH] there. You know what a [NON-ENGLISH] is? No.

speaker: interviewee
text: It's something like a kolkhoz. But a kolkhoz, the people control themselves. A [NON-ENGLISH], everything belonged to the government. But you worked like a defector for the government. But it's farms.

speaker: interviewer
text: So did you-- did you work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's how you had to support yourselves by working like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah-- yeah. Yeah.
